lesson:
The kanji 行 started life as a picture of a crossroads — two paths meeting. In the oldest oracle bone inscriptions, it looked like an intersection viewed from above. It didn't originally mean "to go." It meant the road itself. The meaning drifted over centuries from the place where you walk to the act of walking on it. That's why 行 lives this double life in modern Japanese: as いく it's the verb "to go," but in compounds it often carries its older sense of a path or institution — 銀行 (bank, a "silver firm"), 旅行 (travel, a "journey-going"), 行列 (a procession, people moving along a path).

For the sound: いく is two sharp syllables — i-ku — that feel like they're already moving somewhere. Say it out loud and notice how your mouth opens with い and then snaps shut with く, like a little departure. Now say くる (to come). It starts closed and rounds inward. いく pushes out. くる pulls in. Keep those two as a pair and the sounds start to feel like they mean what they mean.

One thing to watch: 行く's て-form is いって, not the いいて you'd expect. It's the only く-verb in the language that does this. Here's why: 行く used to be ゆく (and you'll still hear ゆく in songs and formal speech). The って form is a fossil of that older pronunciation — ゆく → ゆって, which smoothed into いって even after the main verb shifted to いく. So the irregularity isn't random. It's a piece of the word's history that didn't get cleaned up.

The kanji 見 is ancient. In oracle bone script — the earliest Chinese writing, carved into tortoise shells over 3,000 years ago — it was a drawing of a human figure with an enormous eye for a head. Not a person with an eye. A person who is an eye. The whole body existed to carry that eye around. What survived into the modern kanji is 目 (the eye) sitting on top of 儿 (the legs that carry it). Every time you write 見, you're drawing a little creature whose entire purpose is to look.

Now the sound: miru. Think of the English word mirror. It comes from Latin mirari, "to look at, to wonder at" — the same root as "miracle" and "admire." Japanese and Latin aren't related, so this is pure coincidence — but coincidences make great mnemonics. Let the connection sit there: mi-ru, mi-rror. You look in a mirror to 見る yourself. That's your anchor.

見る does the work of three English verbs. テレビを見る is "watch TV." 景色を見る is "look at the scenery." 夢を見る is "see a dream" — yes, in Japanese you see dreams, you don't have them. The word doesn't care about the distinction between passive seeing and active looking. Context handles it.

The pattern you'll use most: 〜てみる. Attach みる to any verb's て-form and it means "try doing it to see what happens." 食べてみる — try eating it. 聞いてみる — try asking. The logic is transparent: you do the action in order to see the result. Once you notice this pattern, you'll hear it everywhere.

You will, at some point, try to say 多い人 — "many people" — and it will be wrong. Not grammatically wrong in a way anyone can cleanly explain, just wrong. Japanese doesn't let 多い sit in front of a noun like other い-adjectives. 大きい犬 (big dog) is fine. 多い人 isn't. You have to say 多くの人 instead, switching 多い into its adverbial form to make it work. Nobody really knows why this adjective refuses to behave. Its opposite 少ない (few) does the same thing. They're the only two.

Where 多い does work is at the end of a sentence, after が: 人が多い. 最近、雨が多い. 日本は祝日が多い. It describes a situation — "people are numerous," "rain is plentiful" — rather than pointing at a specific noun. Once you get used to that sentence shape, the word stops fighting you.

The kanji 多 is two 夕 stacked. 夕 is a crescent moon — the earliest versions were literally a drawing of a half-moon. Two of them piled up meant "numerous," probably from the idea of many nights passing. It's one of the simpler kanji etymologies but it works: you see double, there's a lot of something.
